intro: Python prioritizes execution abstraction and immediate developer iteration using runtime script evaluation, while Rust guarantees compile-time memory safety, computational execution velocity, and zero-cost abstractions. Converting code to Rust removes runtime garbage collection pauses and interpreter overhead while ensuring thread safety natively. This migration replaces dynamic structural lookups with absolute type ownership rules, explicit lifecycles, and predictive compilation designs.

diffs.from: Strong, dynamic typing. Type checking happens at runtime, allowing variables to change bindings dynamically.
diffs.to: Static, strong type system featuring comprehensive type inference, explicit generics, trait bounds, and algebraic data types.

diffs.from: Whitespace indentation architecture with clean expressions and minimal keyword overhead.
diffs.to: C-style brace syntax containing clear type declarations, macro calls (appending !), and pattern-matching control structures.

diffs.from: Automatically managed via internal reference registries supplemented by a periodic cyclic collector routine.
diffs.to: Governed at compile-time by an ownership tracking model with strict borrow checking rules, eliminating manual allocation and tracking routines.

diffs.from: Single-loop event runtime utilizing cooperative async task handlers inside the asyncio framework package.
diffs.to: Zero-overhead Future model integrated into language syntax, requiring choosing a runtime executor (like Tokio) to scale.

diffs.from: Batteries-included library full of general scripting, file formatting, network routing, and abstract models.
diffs.to: Focused core library providing basic memory primitives, system OS channels, and highly performance collection setups.

examples.title: Safe error propagation via wrappers
examples.description: Compares an exception-raising Python method with Rust's type-safe compile-time Result enum construct.

examples.title: Matching structured enumeration variants
examples.description: Shows Python structural pattern checking matching against Rust's complete compile-time match validations.
examples.fromCode:
def handle_status(status):
    match status:
        case 'ok': return 200
        case 'error': return 500

tips.title: Internalize Ownership and Borrowing
tips.body: Understand that variables take exclusive ownership of values. Pass references using an ampersand (&) to allow values to be read without transferring ownership.

tips.title: Replace Null with Option
tips.body: Rust does not have a null value. Replicate optional data states by leveraging the standard Option<T> enum containing Some(T) and None.

tips.title: Leverage Pattern Matching Natively
tips.body: Use the match control structure for explicit condition routing; the compiler will verify all logic branches are covered.

tips.title: Separate Logic via Traits
tips.body: Replace traditional class inheritance logic by declaring shared interfaces using 'trait' containers, then implementing them for target configurations.

faqs.question: What is the Rust equivalent of a Python dictionary?
faqs.answer: The primary alternative is `std::collections::HashMap`, which requires predefined, fixed compile-time types for keys and values.

faqs.question: Does Rust have a garbage collector?
faqs.answer: No. Rust resolves memory allocation lifecycles at compile time using ownership scope rules, freeing memory the moment a variable drops out of scope.

faqs.question: Can I use Rust to speed up slow Python routines?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can write native extensions in Rust using the `PyO3` framework, allowing you to call compiled Rust functions directly inside Python.
